ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fried soft shell crab; mapped to closest parent crab food product class (FOODON_03302051). A more specific fried soft shell crab term does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crustaceans broadly and is the closest parent available; no specific fried soft shell crab code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for fried soft shell crab; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one whole fried soft shell crab, approximately 80 g, as commonly served in Singaporean seafood restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Soft Shell Crab — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for soft shell crab, fried (NDB 15149) as a secondary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried soft shell crab. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogy with battered/fried seafood and wheat-flour batter coatings. The batter contributes refined carbohydrates (GI ~65 for white wheat flour); the protein and fat content of the crab significantly attenuate the overall glycaemic response, yielding an estimated composite GI of approximately 55. Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (55 × 15.8 × 0.80) / 100 = 6.9 g. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 283 mg/100 g; high calcium 360 mg/100 g partially offsets sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to one crab (≈80 g, contributing ~226 mg sodium) and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as black bean or oyster sauce; the exceptionally high calcium content (360 mg/100 g) provides a vasodilatory benefit consistent with DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (15.8 g/100 g) from wheat-flour batter; protein and fat attenuate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes can consume fried soft shell crab in moderate portions (one crab, ~80 g), as the protein (10.5 g) and fat (15.5 g) slow gastric emptying and moderate the glycaemic response from the batter; requesting a thinner batter or tempura-style coating and avoiding sweet dipping sauces will further reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat; sodium at 283 mg/100 g is borderline for CVD patients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit fried soft shell crab to occasional consumption due to the elevated fat content from deep-frying (15.5 g/100 g total fat, estimated 2.8 g saturated fat) and borderline sodium (283 mg/100 g); choosing steamed or grilled crab preparations would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk while retaining the beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and high calcium content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations of crab as the AGEs generated during deep-frying exacerbate joint inflammation; steamed, boiled, or lightly sautéed crab is strongly preferred to retain the anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefits without AGE formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried soft shell crab is a popular seafood dish in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, consisting of a whole soft shell crab — harvested immediately after moulting when the entire shell is edible — coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior and tender, juicy interior, with the entire crab including legs, claws, and body consumed whole. Per 100 g, it provides 244 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 15.5 g fat, and 15.8 g carbohydrate from the batter coating, along with an exceptionally high calcium content of 360 mg from the edible shell. Sodium is 283 mg/100 g, placing it in the borderline range for hypertension management. The dish is clinically relevant for patients with Type 2 Diabetes due to its moderate carbohydrate load from the batter, though the protein and fat content attenuate the glycaemic response. For cardiovascular patients, the deep-frying method increases total fat and should be consumed in moderation. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this preparation as deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation. It is commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres, seafood restaurants, and zi char stalls, often accompanied by chilli sauce or black pepper sauce.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 for all reported fields. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15149 as a secondary reference. GI is an analogous estimate documented per Veritas GI policy. Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific fried soft shell crab preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry, and no sufficiently close match was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy to supply these values with confidence. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15149 (Crustaceans, crab, soft-shell, fried) and documented as an adapted value. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on battered fried seafood with wheat-flour coating; no direct GI measurement for fried soft shell crab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Dietary tags 'wholegrain' and 'soft_foods' from the input cultural section have been removed as they are not applicable to this deep-fried battered preparation. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from the input has been replaced with more contextually appropriate meal contexts (lunch, dinner, zi char). Calcium value of 360 mg/100 g is notably high and reflects the contribution of the edible shell consumed whole in soft shell crab. Ontology: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as the mapped codes represent parent-level classes; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for fried soft shell crab as of 2024.
